speaker: Albert Pierce
words: I say a little girl, she's a little lady staying and living with a school teacher. And she went somewhere and they made a record of her singing. And when she heard herself, I just got her going. She was a good little singer but it didn't sound good to her. I don't know if it ever sounds good. My voice always sounds funny to me. Yeah, I suppose that's what everybody... Because you can't hear yourself. No, no. Well, the first thing I want to know about is about how the Pierce clan, that whole family, wound up coming out to the dairy country in the first place. I can tell you that pretty easy. I've got that pretty clear in my mind. Yeah, the story should start a little farther back in my head. But I mean mine, I'll tell you and then you know why. My grandfather and grandmother and father and uncle and grandparents all emigrated from the state of Virginia

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: to Minnesota in real early time because I had a cousin. He was an old man when he told me that. He says, you know Albert, I walked all the way from Virginia to Minnesota. He was a young man and all the rest of them were older and they would drive an auction and he had to be at mostly, it seemed like then, I thought that even if you drove auction you could ride. But he said he had to walk almost to their heads. Which then they didn't have, when you stop and think, they didn't have roads. I suppose they had to drive around trees or something. He had to be right at their heads, maybe more than they would if they had a road. Maybe they would follow the road and he could road part of the time. But he had to be right there to lead them. And he says, you know I walked all the way from Virginia to Minnesota.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: And that's where, that was,

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: that was my aunt's son. Granddad Pierce, Grandmother Pierce had three children. My father, Uncle John Pierce and Aunt Rachel Moore all to me because I was Dad's youngest one of the family. And they were all old folks when I could first remember them almost.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they all live, each other in Minnesota?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: They all took up homestead land joining each other.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: In Minnesota?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: In Minnesota, yeah. Hutchison, Minnesota.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well how did they come about to leave Minnesota?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Well that was 60, only 60 miles northwest of Minneapolis. And they lived there till it was all grown and the older ones were all married. The biggest part of, I had two brothers and two sisters. They were married and come out west. And I'm getting a little ahead of my story. It was my uncle who had a place, or he turned the place over. My father, I'm bragging now, I'm not bragging. Go ahead. I'm just glad it happened that way. Dad got 160 acres of land and Uncle John, his brother had got a quarter section, a man right each side. Dad cleared up, oh he had a good farm when he sold it. Never had a mortgage on it and got a long old life. Uncle John didn't have any larger family than Dad did. And got in debt so head over heels, I don't know why he did, or just no management, whatever or what. I just don't know anymore about that, why he got in debt. He told one of his boys, who was a real good fellow, he was ambitious and honest and good every way. He told Joe, if you pay off the mortgage on this place, it's yours. Just give it to him. He gave it to him and he assumed the mortgage that day and he paid it off that day. So I don't know how in the world, he had it all paid off. Good farm too. But it must have been about that time, along about 1900, when there was quite so many people emigrating west, going out. Of course, it took us three days to come on a train from St. Paul to Spokane. So you know, the trains weren't too fast then either. And so they sold their place. Uncle John Pierce had that place. He got in debt and told Joe if he'd pay off the debt, he would give it to him as his. So he did. Then, of course, Joe, being a good fellow, he took care of his parents. In their old days, he'd seen that they were comfortable and looked after them. And his wife was a nice woman too. And so they, I don't know why Joe, when, I think it was some of his friends, none of his relatives, I don't think, came out here around Moscow. Oh yes, I know, it was the Miller man that had the flour mill in Hutchison. He came out and he put in a flour mill at Kendrick because they raised so much wheat on American Ridge, Potlatch Ridge, Tech Ridge, and Bear Ridge, and all around there. And he come back and he talked to so many of his friends about it. And that's when they become interested. So, and it was just about that time when they was up in our country, we lived north of town six miles. There's a lot of young folks out on the prairie come up to buy some of the wooded areas, you know, because they could buy them cheaper than they were to buy all that wood ready for cultivation. And Joe Pierce sold his, that was my cousin, Uncle John's boy. He had taken over Uncle John's place. So he sold it and my uncle, Ed Moore, over on the other side sold it and that sort of got my folks in notion of selling, I guess. And then my oldest sister and her husband, Ed Boger, they came out west. And then when my younger sister, who was six years older than me, she got married, they come west, and I was leaving, father, mother, and I there. So, just as soon as the girls both left, then mother was ready to leave too, you know. So she put on the pressure. Dad was getting up in years and he would have stayed there, but yeah, he was just well off out west as he was back there, the weather was better. And so, he had sold the old place and come out west. He had 160 acres and then he gave my two brothers and my sisters, I think 63 acres. Each one of the boys got 20 and my sister got 13 or something, I don't know, or 23 or something, I forget just what the number of it is. I remember his 163 acres he had. The reason is odd number, he had one lake, so much of it zig -zag around, they'd point out and all that was figured in on taxation, I guess.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Anyhow,

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: my uncle and his son Joe, Joe owned his uncle's place because his uncle got in debt, headed over to the U .S. and turned the Joe and Joe paid it off, I think I told you that, you know. And so they, Joe sold out and he didn't hardly know where he was going and what to do. And some of the folks, neighbors, neighbors I don't think is a relative, I think it's some neighbor, oh yes, it was the Miller in town, the man that had the flour mill, he came out to Kendrick, which is only 23 miles northeast of Lewiston and he put in a flour mill there and he told my dad and he told Joe, too, what a good place, what a good country it was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So everybody wound up coming?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Yes, coming, they talked, one then another.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What do you remember about the train trip?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Well, it took us three days to come from St. Paul to here.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And we had a nice trip but that was like a slow train through Arkansas when you think of it now, wasn't it?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: That's a long time. Yeah, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mentioned to me before there was a little problem with a guy smoking on there?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Oh yes, yes, yes. I never will forget it because I was a kid and it tickled me so. Tobacco smoke always made my mother have a headache. She just couldn't stand it, she around her. And they have a smoking car on all passenger trains like they do even yet, you know, they did them days. And here he come, here come an old fella, sat down just across the street, that wasn't his seat but he just come and sat there and smoked.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: And I don't think Dad said anything to him. Dad might have asked him, told him that it made my mother sick. But he didn't pay no attention. And the old conductor, I saw that this conductor is something I've never seen. I've never seen a conductor or a brakeman on a train with whiskers. He was a young man but he had whiskers down, oh about that long but trimmed and neat and nice. He looked like a neat man and a young man.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: But why would a conductor want to wear whiskers?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: I wonder, chain whiskers. He did. And so Pa told him and his two men come and sat right there by him and blowing that blue smoke wind and making mother sick. And Pa told them that, my dad told the conductor that it made her sick. And he went in a nice way and told them that and asked them to go and, there was a smoker just waiting, told them to go in there. They paid no attention, stayed right there in school and didn't say yes or no or nothing. And he went on the line I suppose one way and then when he came back in there they sat in there smoking.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: And I don't know whether he took time to ask them to go again but anyhow I remember just as well as yesterday him grabbing this man that sat next to him and yanked him out of the seat just as quick as you told him to get into the smoke room. And the second man out as he went, before he went through the door he all along kicked him in the seat of the trousers. He was boss of the train you know. The conductor, he's in charge of the engineer even.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Did you know that?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: I've heard that said. Yeah, the conductor, he has charge of the complete train. If anything goes wrong with it all, he's the business.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: That's why he put him into the other coach right thing. Well listen, when you got out there, you got out to the dairy country, it was Texas Ridge that… That's where we went up to first. It was Texas Ridge just north of Kendrick you know. Kendrick is twenty -three miles northeast of Lewiston. Well, see my brother -in -law and sister came there and he got a place. They liked it. And then when my younger sister and her husband got married, they went out there too and that took our two girls, the daughters. Well then mother, the jig was all up, mother had to go too. And we sold out and went out there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You said that your father had a little difficulty figuring out where he was going to live there though, right? Didn't it take him a while to sell down on that place?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Oh, I know what it was I was going to tell you. When he first come there, he told us, and I don't know, maybe it's other folks around and stuff, he wasn't going to do like most of them come there and go and buy a place first thing. He was going to look around and get just exactly what he wanted.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: I'm telling you just exactly as it was, we went up just six miles north of Kendrick, up on Texas Ridge, which is a nice country up there. And there's American Ridge to the west and Potlatch to the east and there's a lot of good country around there. But dad, he wasn't there a week until someone told him about Juliaetta. Juliaetta was only four miles south of Kendrick down there. It's nice, the climate was good and they bragged up for him. Dad went down there. I don't know whether he looked at more than one place but he bought the poorest place that he could have bought. After said he was going to look around and get thirty acres, of course, he came out alright because he traded it, that thirty acres, for eighty acres way up full, twelve, sixteen, seventeen, eighteen, twenty miles north of there. Then he sold that place for the money so he got his money back. He didn't alright after all. But when he bought that place down there, there was a hundred and sixty acres per sale up on Texas Ridge. And my brother -in -law and my sister wanted eighty acres, that's all he wanted. So dad told him he'd take the other eighty acres, so they bought that. And we never lived in Juliaetta, more in a year or two. And there were no buildings on that eighty acres so he went up and had a house built on it and lived there the rest of his days.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: That the first thing he did there? What did he do when he first got out of the place? Built the house first of all?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Yeah, yeah. And it was only about ten acres cleared up on it and that was all slashed so he had a slashed and the bull pine was laying in rows so he had a man to burn that. He knew how to burn it, knew when to burn it and just how to handle it. And he had that broke up. And just lived on that place the rest of his days, I mean rest up until he got real old and moved into town.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well what did the land, what did the country look like to you when you first saw it?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: I was only a kid about sixteen years old and I just loved it. And back east, the only horses I had would be one of the old horses, two old trot for near you know, or and I had one crooked legged colt and I was a horse lover from the time I ever seen one. I think I loved the horse when I was a baby. And so we, where am I, got all my story now. Oh, you were talking about liking horses and I had asked you what the country was like to you when you first got there. Well that just appealed to me because all the young folks, boys and girls, every one of them had saddle horses. And do you know what I, maybe I've told you, what I bought was one colt or two, one or two colts for one time, two dollars a piece. I mean yearling, year old colt. Yes sir. Nice colt, not one thing that mattered with it. And then I could buy, well I bought a nice little saddle horse, white horse. You had to look up when you got on her if you wasn't a rider. And I was never a good rider but I got so, I wasn't afraid of her. She wasn't mean, she just tried to throw you off and if you could stay on her for a little bit, that's all there was to it, she'd get back up. And I bought her for seven dollars and with a good riding right along. And horses are cheap and that just appealed to me. So I like that Western country just appealed to me more. Did my old daddy, I don't think, poor old dad was too old to adjust to different place, different conditions and everything. Although he bought, he bought, he had two places up there one time. He had 80 acres on two different places and someone come along by the other place that we, we had, my grandfather and grandmother was living on it and he sold it. Just kept 80 acres.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well what did you do for amusement? What did the kids do? Did the kids have a good time together?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: I tell you, I think the kids, them days, young folks them days had better times than they do today. Too much of this is getting, these young folks now getting in the car and drive about a hundred miles, don't know what they're going there for or what they're going to do when they get there or go to a show and that's about it. Well them days, all the boys and all the girls and family, if they struggled, they all had saddle horses. And always quarreling about, each one had the best horse, you know. And, and now let's see other things that they, there's one thing too out in the country, dad had, lived on the farm out four, five miles. And, of a winter time they had literaries or debates in public school houses. And you take the good public speakers, they'd always decide on some question that was debatable, you know. And, and then they would have songs and different, different things for people to do, for entertainment. And when I think of it, I believe them young folks them days had better times than people, young folks have today. I believe they really enjoyed themselves, just as much or more.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about raising Cain? Did you tell me that, that, that they used to play like tricks on Halloween, that sort of thing sometimes?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Oh yes, Halloween all the time. That, that was carried on for years and years and years. Why it was carried on, well, I think wife and I was married 20 years before they ever stopped.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Are there any that you remember from the old, any of the old tricks they used to pull?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Oh yes, mostly. Take out in the country they had these outhouses, you know, always going to turn them over. And right in the town of Erie one time, the editor's wife was sitting in, in the outside toilet when some, some of them folks come, they didn't know she was in there. Probably he or she might have thought afterward that they thought that, but they didn't know. I don't, I don't remember who it was at all now, but they turned that thing over. I don't know whether she fell or how, what position she was in when it went over. So there was a lot of funny, didos, pulled them days too, you know.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: And I guess there was a lot of other things that I've almost forgotten, haven't thought of.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you tell me that kids used to go and climb Spud Hill?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Oh yeah, yes. That was, that was a, I just thought now how I used to climb that old Spud Hill.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: And I carried after, after our baby was born, and I carried him up there one time. I believe I carried my niece, who is going to be married in a few days now, or for a second time. Her first husband died, oh, long, 10, 12, 15 years ago, she will be married. I carried her up that hill one time, and one, I remember my niece, when she said, oh, Uncle Albert, I put her down and I said, I'll take you by the hand. I was getting tired before I got up the top. But she said she just couldn't, so I had to carry her all the way up. That was a hard little job.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well, in the real old days, could you ride your horse a lot of the ways up there?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Oh yeah, we could ride them almost all the way up there. One of them we had go about a mile east of Deary, and go up, then we had what we call a hogback, a slope, just a sliding slope, from way back here to almost to the top. We could tie our horses to the tree, and there would be no object in taking the horses right top, because top was all rocks, and a horse would have to just pick his place, almost have him place his foot in the right place because he's rough. And, there's about 30 feet, or 30 or 40 feet, 50 feet down to where the trees were when we dined there and walked. There's a trail there, and I can remember the first times up there, and it was in the spring, on Easter Day in 1905, there was about seven or eight of us, eight I think, eight or nine went up, and they had built a monument, piled one rock, a small rock on top of another, and built it up about six feet high, and it was, I don't know who done it, and everybody went up there, made a peg, about that long, and would print their name on it, put their name on it. I don't think it was just lead pencil, I think they must have carved it with a knife so it would last. And, the pegs, the pegs are stuck in there, the wind would blow down, or someone would knock them down, and I bet you they were that thick all around that foot or two. These pegs were like pieces of wood. Yeah, like little pieces of wood, just like you've seen these in that bale of hay, little pegs, something like that. They'd probably make it out of green wood or something, I don't know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So what was the view of the country like?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Oh beautiful, you could see clear down, there's two mountain peaks, oh no, that isn't there. You could see a long, long way, I'm going to tell you, you could see two mountain peaks down in Oregon, but that's up here on Mount Focan. But you could see a long, long way from Mount Deary, or we call it Spud Hill, they changed it. Well you saw the town side of Deary before there was any town there. Nothing but a log cabin, log cabin, and the first time I was there, log cabin, and a little old barn, that was all the way in Deary. Yes, sir.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Whose place was that?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Called the name of Blalock, homesteaded. And the town side company, I think it was a warehouse or lumber company bought the town side, but they put a fellow, Fred McGowan and Hugh Henry there to promote it. They come up and had it surveyed in the lots and sold our lots and stayed up there until they got all the lots sold. And then they pulled out for, do the same work somewhere else, I suppose.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did they do that for Potlatch, for the company?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Yeah, yeah. Or whoever owned the, yes it was them that had it. Yeah, because anyone that bought lots after that, after that, after they pulled out, and had sold just about all the lots, they had to buy the lots from some individual that had bought. Some people bought them for speculation or for some future use and probably change their mind. So there was never an old, there's always lots and lots for sale.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Were most of the people that bought the lots local people or were most of them from out of the area?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Well, an awful lot of the business people are from out of the area. He's an old Norwegian fellow and he was Norwegian by blood, but if there ever was one by nature, he was a Jew. Cool. He would sell goods. I don't know whether he ever put a price on it, one price on it, goods or not. He was good enough to judge human nature, know who.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: And, hmm.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: All spring up overnight, did it?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Well, yes, he'd come up in a hurry, just like mushrooms. But of course, some of them there, and businesses didn't make it, and others kept coming in.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: And, hmm. I can remember when they had about twenty horses there in the livery barn and they were doing a good business. And then, of course, just as soon as the cars come, the livery barn went out and the garages and service stations started.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about Swan Erikson? Will you tell me what you remember about him?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Oh, poor old Swan was just the finest old Swenski. He was from Sweden when he was a young fella. He told me one time, he said, you know,

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: one time when I was coming over across the water, he says, I got so sick, I had stomachs sick, you know, ski sick. He said, I got so sick, I thought sure I was going to die.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: And, everybody life goes on, on the stove fella, never married. And, he built a good big blacksmith shop there, and he built living quarters up in front, upstairs, right in front, in the front part of his. And, he was over at our place, our store, in there visiting the last place he was ever visited. He'd come every evening visiting around different people because he's over to his shop all day, you know, and he liked to get out and visit people. And, he went up to the other store before he'd come down to our place, they said, that night. And, then our place was the last place, so when I got ready to close, he went home, and he had quite a long stairway to go up to his living quarters. And, he had some wood split up into, that's when they burned wood in the cookstoves, you know, in the cookstove size. So, he just filled his arm full and went and carried it up. Evidently, that was about all, just all it took, carrying it up there. And, he didn't get it quite in the wood box. He got it just about wood box, and his arm went down and went on the floor, and so on and so on. He was just as good a fella, honest as day was long, good, loved kids, a bachelor, and every kid in town would swear by him because he was just so good to them. And, no matter where they'd seem, anywhere, why, they'd all call him Swan. Hello, Swan. Even our boy, I don't believe our boy is taller than that, when he knew Swan Erikson called him by his first name, and all other kids did too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did he give the kids presents?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Oh, Jim and Nichols. They didn't like him just for that one reason, because he was just good to them, and they liked to watch him pound out that red -hot iron and make this and make that, and liked to watch him be off the waves and watch him shoe horses and everything. And, as I say, he was, I remember one time, he'd give all the kids in town a pen, and our boy, probably seven years old, he'd give him a pen, a writing pen.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Hadn't hardly learned to write. Maybe he was only about six years old. I don't think Arlen could write yet, but I guess we burned out in town. Our place of business burned out, the whole block burned. One time that tree, and we lost that, but we probably had that pen yet.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Well, Swan moved up from Troy to Deary.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: I wonder if he had much feeling. I wonder why he moved to Deary. Well, I tell you, see, I don't know how long he'd been in the blacksmith business in Troy, but he, there wasn't, and I think there was another blacksmith shop there. I think there's two blacksmith shops there then. And there was no two, and Swan wanted to, he had a sister living up close to Deary. I think maybe that might have been one reason he wanted to get up there, and another place where he would have been the only barber shop. Well, for a while there were two barber shops in Deary too. Two barber shops, blacksmith shops in Deary, but the other two fellas, they left. There were two brothers. I think just one of them worked most of the time, the other one worked somewhere else.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that Deary was a lot like Troy as a town? Do you think the towns were pretty similar as towns?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Well, pretty much. Both farm towns, that's what they were. Troy did have a little more, they had a clay brick plant there. Troy had that much more than Deary.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: They started a brick plant there. The banker done that, and if he'd let someone else done it, or tried to sold out, but when he seen he wasn't going to make it too good at it, well, he just let it go.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: And, and,

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: let's see. Well, that was about all that Troy had that... Deary didn't. Yeah, yeah. So Deary was pretty lively place then. Yes, of course, Deary burned out. That crypt set it back one time, of course it made it.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: I was in business there then, and hardware man, Mr. Curtis, we come up, I had some business in town, well, and he did too. So we come up to town, and his father lived out, just out of town ways. First little town out here. I forget the name of it. I don't think it's there anymore.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is this Spokane area?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Came up to Spokane. Yeah, and next morning we'd gone home and I got on a train, just got on a train, I met a woman, a mother -in -law of a man that run a confectionary right alongside of our place. We had a general store, and I says, I asked her how she was, or the folks was, or something like that, and she said, well, Mr. Pierce, I bet you haven't heard what's happened to Deary.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: I says, what?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: She said it burned to the ground last night. Well, in there I left my wife and baby living in the back of the store. I was, well, first town I got out in could, where they stopped long enough that I could phone. I phoned then, and they told me just was what, not to worry, because I didn't know what in the world might have happened. Of course, if I knew someone was killed or something, I would have heard somehow, or they'd sent someone up or something.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: That's the way that was.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you lost the business?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Yeah, lost business then. Then I started up again in business. The old fellow that I was renting the building from, the frame building, he put a brick building for me. I stayed with him another nine, seven or nine years, which I sold out.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And what kind of store was it?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: General store.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: How did you do this? How was it to be in business there in Deary?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: It wasn't too good. We had three stores there then. And then afterward, one of them fellas went out, so it was two stores. It was a good place for two stores. I would have liked to have one then, been one of them. But when there was three of us, I ended up three, because I don't care who it is. If he's a black man, he would get some of the business anyhow. He'd run a sale or something or do something else. You feel it, you know.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was it, for running a business, was there any trouble with it being in the farm community? Did you have to wait on harvest?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: That was one thing that I didn't like about it.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: The only thing that I didn't like about it. You had to wait too long, from spring till fall, or so many of them you got, until they harvested their crop. And that works a hardship. It takes all your profits to put right back in the business. And if you're not pretty careful, you're going to let too much slip through your fingers, you know. It's going out faster than it comes in. But we knew everybody, and that made it a little bit worse if you were among strangers. You had,

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: if they knew that you'd given someone a little credit, why they, their neighbor would feel as though they were entitled to it too.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: So I can see why Jews that I knew way back, a long time, just soon get out and clear out, among where they didn't know anyone to be in business. And I believe that is one reason they could treat everybody just like a nut, getting difficulty over it. Well I want to go back and work back before Deere started and ask you some more about the old times. Alright, I can get back to, I can get back about nineteen -five as far as I can go. Okay, well, okay first, first I'd like you to locate where the Blalock Homestead was. Right, the town site of Deere was Blalock, Blalock, part of the Blalock Homestead.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where would it be today if you were looking in Deere for that, that site, the old first homestead?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Well right down Main Street is right in that old homestead site. That splits, that almost splits Deere in two.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: And then out to one side, I don't know whose old homestead it was, but Stockwell's. Mr. Stockwell had passed away, I never knew him, but Jimmy Stockwell, then he had another brother, and their mother, and some girls all lived in a house over on the, big white house on the east side. That house disappeared a long time ago, it must have burned. And that was all, that was all to one side of Deere, and of course Deere goes up to it. And, and when I first went there, when I first saw Deere, town site, town site, was there was two, four, six, eight, about ten of us. On Easter Sunday in nineteen, either four or five, I've almost forgotten which it was, to tell the truth. I get up on a stand and testify to the truth, like couldn't hardly say just what it was. Well it's only been about seventy years ago now, that's pretty long time. Yeah, and well yes it'd be seventy, if it was four, if it was five, it would be, it would be, but I believe it's four, because we, we was all going up on Spud Hill, horseback. We had to go way over east of town, more than a mile east of town, to get up on a, I think, I guess I told you this a while ago though, to go up that hogsback, and tie the horses up there.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And that was the first time that you got a look at it?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: That was the first time I ever got a look from the top of the hill, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about some of these other old homesteaders?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: There was one guy you mentioned to me before named Leising.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Oh yeah, poor old August Leising.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Will you tell me about him?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Yes, I'll tell you about him. He was an old German fellow. He come on Texas Ridge, and they tell me, he had it all cleared when I first met him, but about forty or fifty acres. And they told me that was the heaviest quartered timber, quartered on Texas Ridge. Nice big yellow pine. And he cut a hat, he cut them down, rolled them together up against the stumps and burned them. He had burnt logs, stumps, roots and all. And he just got through the last twenty acres. He had a hundred and forty acres cleared up. He had about twenty acres down in the south, south west corner of his, just like this. This is a course, he had twenty acres right in here wasn't cleared up. It was cleared, but not broke. And he had to cut some small brush out and things like that. And he was a little bit slow in doing that. And he just got that all broke up. I don't know whether he raised a crop on it or not when he died. So he just got his hundred and sixty acres, and everybody put it up and cleared up.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: He was a come from Germany. He was just as honest as the day was long. And one time something happened with his eye and kept getting worse. You see great things, big reds spot in. So he went to Portland, it was the cancer of his eye. He went to Portland, I think he had that eye taken out. And, yeah, you know, I don't think it, I know it.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: He come back, he come back with, come back, I had a story there, and he come in and when there wasn't anyone in, he says, Albert, could you take this eye out so I can clean it?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Well, I says, I don't know whether I could or not, August, but I'll try. And you know, we had one little corner curtain off where women went in to try dresses. We sold women's dresses and hats. And so my wife helped me and my daughter and all that.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: And when I turned back in that dressing room, and I was afraid I might hurt that. He come back with a glass eye, they took that eye out, and I was afraid to touch it much harder, I was afraid I might hurt him. And I got it, thought I'd get it and just turned it alongside two, just made him look awful. And someone come in the store, August said, go take care of him, go take care of him. So I had to leave him there. Someone that walked in, see him, he walked out, he frightened him by that.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What did he do?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean he did walk out?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: No, he didn't, I said he would have. And after whoever went out, anyhow, I got straightened out, but who had to teach us job, I didn't want to do it again. And afterward, the mailman, Axel Brandland, was carrying mail, go by every day, and I think he paid Axel to stop. Axel was one of these money makers, you know, he'd do anything extra to make some money. And I think that every day or every other day he'd come and take that eye out, and August came. I think August could put it back in himself, it's just taken out. It looked like he could have learned and done that too, but he didn't for a long time. Maybe he did last, but for a long time, I know the mailman done that. But he was from Germany, a little short, heavy as ever. And a heavy as quard timber, and a timbered section land, and he cleared it all up, just got it all cleared every bit of it when he passed away.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Speaking of the store, when you mentioned that, I forgot to have asked you about what you did sell in there, and what sold good and what didn't sell good in the 20s when you had it, do you remember?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Well, we had more groceries than anything else, but we got quite a little of the general store. Like overalls and gloves,

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: and so many clothes for men. And then my wife sold quite a lot, she was selling a lot of these. Well, they sent them to us on consignment from Made in Seattle, some dress company. That's the way they were putting them out.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: He went back to Germany one time, had quite a visit back there. He built a good house, funny thing. He built a good house, and the fellow name was Burt Baker, neighbor there. He was going over there, I was glad it happened to him, because he almost had it coming. It's raining and storming, and poor old August before he built this nice, good, big new house, he just had a little old log shanty, and just not fit for a bachelor to live in, you know. But anyhow, he come in, and Burt Baker come over to visit him, and it's raining, and his feet was muddy too, he was going to walk by, and August made him go out and clean his shoes all off before he could come in the house.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: And he went back to Germany one time for a visit, made quite a visit back there, I guess the first time he'd been back there for a long time. And then he took awful sick. My brother -in -law, Ed Boger and I, went and set up different neighbors, we'd go and set up with him at night, you know. And that's before he ever went to hospital, he never did recover from that sickness, because when he got worse, they took him to the hospital, and he died in the hospital. But while we were there, him and I was staying that night, and August took worse. He got to feel bad, and he commenced to talk about, he didn't know just what would happen to his property. You know, property meant awful lot to him. And foreigners, when they'd come and got it for nothing, cleared it up and everything, that meant, it was something, but... And so, well, Ed says, you've got, see he went back before the war with Germany. And when he'd come back, he said he didn't know why he did well. And my brother -in -law says, August, you've got relatives back, and he says, I don't know whether I have or not now. Because we'd been at war with them, and England was at war with them, and they'd probably all kill. He hadn't heard many of them for a long time. And I believe they were, because a fellow, a young German man, he was made administrator, so he disposed of it, and he got in touch. If he had any relatives at all left over in Germany, why, they got their share of it, and if they didn't, why, it was distributed by law in a way it should have been.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think that he had any harder time because he was German during World War I?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: No, no, no, no, no, there wasn't, because August, he was one of these fellows just as honest as the day was long. And he probably, should I say, and it might be wrong to be, he probably wouldn't care which one, this country or Germany. He probably wouldn't make any difference to him. He probably had just this much feeling for his home, home country where he was born and raised, and all of his ancestry was. He probably wouldn't have felt just this happy, but he never expressed himself that way, so he never got any, if God, no one ever suspected him at all, because he paid his taxes, and he was just a good neighbour, good man, good person. Everybody, everybody, no one would, if you could talk to, there's not very many fellows around you could talk to now at that noise, but if there's very many of them around, they'd all tell you just the same story that I am at August, was it, and so on. Ralph LeBeau lived down in Texas, yet he's, oh about two years younger than I am, but he was one of these quiet fellows, just say there's something you want to find out, real badly about August. You go there and ask him, he probably wouldn't tell you, so, just a little bit odd, he's a bachelor, never got married.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Is there anybody else like, who stands out in your mind, these old timers like August, who were sort of special, they stood out as being, oh not exactly a character, but different kind of person, that really was an individual, like August or Swan Erikson, is there other people around there you remember from the old times?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Well, now let's see, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you go to the hospital for a few years?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: No, I didn't go home, man. I didn't say he was, I was just wondering if he was, I can't remember. No, it's not Mackie, there could be some other old fellow there, but there's never a Mackie lived there the whole time.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Tom and Al Lebow were old timers, but they weren't old men, you know, they got in there early and got homesteads, they must have come when they were 21, 22, or 23 years old and got homesteads, so there wasn't such a, let's see.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: There was buyers, both buyers, they were German fellows, come from Germany, and Riley's were old timers.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: And Mackie, like you said,

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: yeah, yeah. And Al Mackie didn't live to be such an old fellow, but his father owned 2 ,000 acres of land, and then when he got to be so old to live alone, he come and lived with Mr. and Mrs. Al Mackie, after their children were grown. And that was, I remember, sometimes you know, I'm getting so, my memory isn't as good as it used to be. And I went there one time to see Al Mackie for something, and he was waiting for home, but going to be back right away, and they told me, come on in and wait a while. Well there, the old Mr. Mackie was sitting in there, the old grandpa, a nice old fellow. And so he, when he heard me say something about Dave Oosterhout, it was a man that he had known. And Oosterhout was a pioneer, and Mr. Mackie was an old pioneer, so they would reach out. When he heard me, he was making me, and he said, say, how is Dave Oosterhout, or where is he, or how is he, or something. I told him. And then we sat there, visit, and the other person, about 15 minutes, he asked me the same question again, say how is. It stayed, it kind of hung in his mind, that he'd forgotten that it was just a shorter time. And once in a while now, when I hear something one day, and then each time I, it seemed like it was a lot longer, so I guess I'm getting a little bit that way. But it gets old. Yes, yes.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Well how did he ever get 2 ,000 acres of land?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Well, I tell you, I don't know. In the cattle business, I guess. An awful lot of that land, too, was in the canyon, in the hilly rutland, was only good for pasture. Then he had a lot of good land that he raised grain on, and sold the grain, and then he raised the cattle on, had plenty grazing land, so that took care of the cattle from spring till fall, and they run the straw stacks and saved enough grain to feed them.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Where was most of his land at?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Well, on the point of Bear Ridge. Bear Ridge is just west of Texas Ridge, most of it on there. And I think he had a little on Texas Ridge, too, but most of it was on Bear Ridge. And then his son, Alumeti, had, well, he had, I guess he had as much land as his dad did, but he had his on Texas Ridge. And then, of course, he got all of his dads, too, so he was just so rich that him and his wife had to split up, and his daughter and his son wouldn't, and him wouldn't, weren't on friendly terminal at all. So that's what's well done for that family. Miss McKee, his wife is a good girl, was a good woman, his daughter was a good woman, and I guess the boy turned out to be a good dog, too.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You think money can make it pretty hard on you?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Too much money, too much, yeah. And of course, it wasn't all money there, because he's one of these fellas, he's riding horseback all the time, traveling all the time, buying and selling cattle, and no matter what people had to buy or sell, why he'd buy or sell, he knew where to make money on it.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: You told me before about a killing down on the point, near the point of one of the ridges.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What was that story?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Well, that was before my time, but I'll tell you about that one.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: There's a fellow by the name of McCoy, I knew Bill McCoy and Mace McCoy, both good fellows, but I didn't know this one. And I knew Huey Stanton, Harry Stanton, and Claude Stanton, and their mother, but I didn't know the father. Well, them two fellas lived right down on the point, Stanton's place was right on the point, they had a good quarter section land, had they owned it, yes, Stanton Dam, they did, unless it had been sold the last fifteen, twenty years, maybe, Dad. Oh yes, I know, I heard the woman bought it. But, they had some difficulty over something, disagreement. So, this man, McCoy, come down to see Mr. Stanton, and it was into his house, and they talked it over, and they just agreed to disagree and go be friends from now on. And, cut off that nonsense of being enemies at a sword point and everything. And, now then, I don't know, surely, Stanton, I wonder if he would be carrying a revolver in his pocket at that time, when he knew this man comes seeing. He might have done that very thing. He might have done that very thing. For, anyhow, they settled their disagreement, and he started to walk out from the house to the road, and Stanton went walking along with him. Well, they said, talking like this, well, now, they're both glad that they come to an agreement, and go be friends. And, this one said, well, now you, I wouldn't know the word, but you behave yourself, or something like that from now on. He didn't make it weird. He pinned it on the other fellow. He asked the other fellow, he says, you do the same, or something worse. And, one word led to another, and Stanton shot him dead. That was a murder on that face. There's one or two others, but I've just forgotten.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: This, oh, no, that was them, two other fellows murdered on the, from Tex Ridge, but it was elsewhere, where they were, met their federal rules. One was down to Kendrick, in Kendrick town, and the other one, this fellow's only about a year or two older than me that was killed downtown.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And, uh, in Kendrick?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What, what, do you remember what happened to him, that he died? In Kendrick?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Well, I tell ya, he was just the best little hard worker, and a good fellow every way, in his young days. And, when he was up, I think, being quite an old bachelor, he married a young girl. And, then, I don't know whether she, she left him. I don't know whether it was for some other fellow, or something. And, uh, and,

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: uh, anyhow, he was down, he lived up on Texas Ridge, and went down to Kendrick, and, I don't know, he was rambling around there at night, I don't know whether he went to drink, he wasn't a drinking man, when he was a young fellow at all. But, I believe someone said he went to drink in there, the last one, he was fighting with his wife. And, uh, he, uh, he went up and pounded on a saloon door. And, I hate to tell this story, because it's him, he wouldn't go in the saloon, he was just as good a fellow as you, you're fine, anywhere. Ambitious, and good worker, and fell on fine, but, uh, he knocked on the door, and, uh, I, I, saloon keeper told him, go away, or something. And, he, he died, probably so drunk, he didn't know what he was doing, went and pounded, second or third time, and a saloon man shot him. Killed him right there at the door. That was one, then there's some other one, but I, that there slipped my mind.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Who was the guy?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Uh, Bill Darr. Bill Darr. Bill Darr. Yeah. Uh, uh, uh.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did this Stanton ever get in trouble for what he'd done? Did he ever get, uh, called in?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Oh, he killed Stanton.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You said he killed that fella in that fight, but did he ever get in trouble for that?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: That, that's what I told you that I wasn't too familiar with.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: With what happened? Yes, I don't know, do you remember?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Now, my, I know, my, uh, sister, my sister and her husband was the next folks that lived on that place, he ran that place, and I should know. But I did, and I knew Harry Stanton, Huey Stanton, Clark Stanton, all them, but I never, I don't know what Stanton got. Uh, and, and I don't know the nature of the trouble, what caused it. He was on the Stanton place when it happened, you see. So if he was there for trouble, uh, That was before you got there. Before, I never seen Mr. Stanton. I knew all the boys afterwards, but I, I, and, and it might have been at that time I learned, I don't, maybe I never did hear just what.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: There was a third, there was a third murder, too.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: There was another guy from, from, uh, Texas where he got killed, and you don't really remember how that happened. Well, there was another one, the name of Bud Stroll, he went to California, he was killed there. And then there's still another one, and I, right now I can't hardly think, there's three, three colors that was murdered in there. And I can't think who that third one was, the second or third one, anyhow.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Well, the one thing I wanted to ask you about that I haven't yet is this business when the railroad came in and then they started cutting the timber.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of deal was it that Potlatch made with the, the farmers about cutting that timber?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: I'll tell you. The, the Potlatch Company got the timber given to them. Because if they bought, if they bought the timber, it would cost them as much, and logged it themselves, all that, it would cost them as much as a farmer hauled into the right -of -way. Three dollars, three dollars a thousand for nice, big bogs. And a dollar, they would pay a dollar a thousand stumping trees. But they, of course, they didn't want to do that, but they got off of that, and because most farmers thought that they could make a little money on logging, which I don't suppose it did, because now there wasn't one farmer and another that knew anything about logging, you know, or had logging equipment.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you tell me that you got in, you tried that once?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Yeah, my brother -in -law and I, we logged our own timber off and for our neighbor, but we never made any money on it. We thought we were going to make good money.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What happened when you tried?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Well, we went ahead, we got all of our logs in, but when it counted all up, we didn't make good wages, we didn't make halfway good wages, hardly. We got money for our logs, but didn't make any wages and hauled them in.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did many farmers do the same thing, sell their timber to potlatch?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Yeah, they all, pretty much all of them did, but most of them let the company log it off.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: There was a lot of, was there as much timber land as clear land when the potlatch came in?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Yeah, there's more. There's more timber land than was cleared up land then.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: So potlatch got a pretty good deal. Oh yes, they got a good deal. No wonder though, where else would lumber come, that's for rich. Of course, they were rich a long time ago, but they kept getting richer and richer and richer fast.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Because that's what happened there and that's what happened every place. They went into a new place, you know, where they're a little out of the way.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you think that people felt sorry later that they just gave it away or didn't they have any choice?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Well, I'll tell you here, most of them got a chance to get a hold of a little money then, you know, that they wouldn't have otherwise. They'd had to hell it and some of them folks that owned it was getting up for years and they thought if they ever derived any benefit out of it, they'd better be selling it then.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you tell me that potlatch came, did you say they tried to sell them cars afterwards?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: You didn't, they didn't try, they done it. They had a fella, got a fella down in the potlatch who was a car salesman. Who was a car salesman. Excuse me. Sure.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: They had a potlatch sent on a car salesman. Yeah, sent a car salesman right up after. Just they sold a lamp, sent him up to, and he done it too. He sold the biggest majority of them a car.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You mean after they logged the land?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Yeah, after they logged the land, was getting the money. They had it all timed just right so they didn't want to sell them in the car if they didn't have any money. But just when the money was there, it was already there. Just like a cat punching on a mouse.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So, do you think this guy came right from potlatch who was selling the cars?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Yes, he did. He came right from potlatch.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What kind of cars were they selling?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Ford. And to tell the truth, that's the only cars that they had right then and then before, in a little while, while you had the Maxwell.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But when was this in the Ford's, selling the Ford's, do you remember? Why do you hear?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Yeah, just rough. I don't expect to tell you exactly. Yeah, that's one thing I could give you now about. Well, in 19... 19... Oh, it doesn't matter. Oh, I tell you, it didn't, about 1914 I believe. I believe that's about as early as we got the Ford's out here. I think it was. And they had them then. So, it's 19... just about 1914 I think.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about Joe Wells? Did you know him?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Sure, I knew old Joe Wells.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Know any stories about Joe? Anything about him? What he was like?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Yeah, it's just as well let not be told.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: He was a hard, hard old worker in his days. And, but, old, old alcohol didn't make him a better man.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Well, I've heard that before. His last later years.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Did you go to Lou's funeral?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: I did and I think my wife did. Everybody liked her. She's a good old soul.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What about the Indians? Did you see them in the early days?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Oh, all kinds of them. They come up right by our place all the time. Every, from Spring to Fall all the time. But then they were just, they were civilized Indians. Never tried to, never tried to... You know, here's where you'd travel up. Say if this is a hill, mountain here. You'd go up and it's too steep to go up. You'd go up here and make a switchback, make a turn here. You'd go up the hill that way, you know.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: And, if you'd meet an Indian and if you're going down, you know, you're supposed to, if you're going down the hill, you would be on the right hand side, so you should be on the inside. But, Indian always had a bad horse. A bad horse. He couldn't get out on the outside.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: And they just thought that they'd put, putting that over on everybody. Everybody didn't take it. Once or twice, they could have done it, but they'd done it all the time. And, they was afraid to get out on our edge of that bridge.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Was your mother afraid of Indians?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: No, no. When we came west in 1904, or where were they in 1940, Indians were just... They were civilized. They were just firemen. They were just firemen like white people, all dumb there. Some of them was. Not very many of them was farmers, I guess, because they all got allotments. Got enough money to live from the government, you know. But, you take some of the younger fellows, they were just, they were good farmers. I know a farmer named McDonald, Indian, lived down below Juliaetta, about four, four or five miles below Juliaetta. Why, he had, he was farming hundreds of acres of land, way back early. So, they was ambitious people among them, way back, long time ago, too. Not very many of them, I bet you, you couldn't say that about very many Indians, but he was one that, perhaps, his father would give him a good boost, and he was ambitious, and he just made it fine.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Have you got any feeling about there being, when the best times were in Deary, or when life there was best?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Well, I suppose, it's when there's logging, taking logs off all around Deary. You know, I'll tell you one thing, maybe I told you. Old man warehouser, old friend, old grandpa, daddy of them all, you know, granddaddy of them all, and they was logging by air, you know. They had, they put up all these square, like square posts, you know, like you see, wide at the bottom and narrow up top. They had them, and then, they were the posts for the cables, and would have great big steel baskets, should I call them.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Like, did you ever see them haul grain up and down a hill on them?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Just beyond your time, young man, yeah. Well, that's the way they was. They would make great, big, strong steel things to lay the logs on, and they was logging them that way, over some rough land out there. And old Frederick warehouser, though, the man himself, come out and looked at them, they said, he looked up at them. Well, he said he was glad of one thing. He's glad that he lived long enough to see them logging in there. He'd made his fortunes and fortunes, you know, logging, but he was just glad he'd seen that. He'd never, he'd seen something then that he never expected to see.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you think the Deary went downhill after the fire?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Yes, it did.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: And not so much after the fire, it was going down, because after they got all the timber off, we just hadn't depended on the farming industry. It had become a farmer town, which is, that's just about all it is now. Of course, there's quite a few fellows living in town that go clear up to help river work, in the woods or this place, that place, I think, where my nephew's is doing that now, my nephew's son. And I believe he drives back, he got in the new car, and he drives back and forth every day.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Do you remember the Burke place?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Oh yes, Arthur Burke, I remember him back and forth, not as far as I remember anything. And if we were talking about him not long ago, I didn't think he was living yet. Yes, they say he is, yeah. I remember his old dad, his dad, if I remember right, had one eye out. Yeah, and I remember one time I was, I had a load of fruit, I'd taken a load of fruit I guess to Bovill, and I had a team of horses, I had an awful nice team of horses, but they was lively, fast walkers, and you had to, instead of, it was one of these teams you had to hang on to the lines all the time, instead of urging them at all. And down by their barn, I had to open a gate and let them through, then shut the gate, and Arthur Burke and his old dad were setting up on the porch, their house was up on the old, equation barn. And so when I got out and opened the gate, and I'd just forgotten whether I went and led them through or how. Anyhow, I went back after they got through the gate, when, I always did train horses, I love horses, I just love them, but I'd never mean to them, and I always trained them to stop when I say stop without jerking them, a lot of people would jerk a horse, hurt their mouth, I never did do that. And so I got the team through and went back to shut the gate, and something happened, I believe there was a colt there, and he jumped over a fence or run through there or something, and kept fighting, and I yelled, whoa, whoa, whoa, treetime gestures were sharp and loud, and the horses stopped right there. And so then here come Arthur Burke and his old dad down there said that's the best they ever seen, horses buying, well they were spriting and starting to run and then stop, because I just hollered, whoa. They was lively, and I was just about as nice a team as I ever had, they were 12 and 13 looked alike, and gated alike, fast, and only you had to look out because they were just ready to go, at any time or anything happened while they were ready to go.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: What led you out of farming and into starting this movement to it?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Well, I tell you, I was never a farmer's heart, never was. And I only had 60 acres, and that wasn't very much, I didn't like it, so I was married, and I thought that would be better than a store business. I worked that little bit in my younger days, it was all the experience I'd ever had before over on the coast.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: But I was never, I'm never sorry I got off the farm when I did.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: And when you decided to quit the mercantile, did you decide to leave Erie at the same time?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: No, I got a job with Sears -Roma, that's the reason I came up here, that's the reason I left Erie. I had sold out, and I was helping some woman there for a while, and I got a job at Sears -Roma. I was a little girl, I was a little girl, I was a little girl, I was a little girl, I was a little girl. I didn't know how to look. And just to see, I thought I'd try it and see how I liked it. And I liked it real well, so we sold out, sold our place, we didn't sell our place. We had two places down there, and we sold, we didn't sell either one for quite a little while, to see whether we liked Spokane well enough to make it from home, but we did. We sold both places down there.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: We're gonna have to get going, we're gonna have to get going, because it's almost quarter after one, and I'm supposed to be someplace at two. Exactly! Goodness sakes!

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: How fast time does fly! I think I was there one time, and someone, I think maybe someone kind of smiled at him. Well, he laughed too. He said, you know, most everybody's life is interesting, their life story, even yours.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: So you think mine would be interesting too?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Yeah, I bet you would be too, even as a young man. If you could just hit enough high spots close enough, not skip two longer spaces in between, it'd be interesting too.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: It's funny, I think about that now and then, but I don't think I have my story down yet. I think maybe when I've lived a lot longer, I will. Yeah, yeah, yeah, yes you will. But now it doesn't seem very interesting to you.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Well, some things aren't interesting. When I think of some of the hardships I went through, I remember one year. I remember one year, and that was then a fella's fault, but mine too, I thought I had more sense. I had a cousin that always, he didn't care how hard the other fella's job was, or working for him, or wouldn't, he just didn't care. He wanted the other fella to do most of the work, that's it. And I went to work for him one time, him and his brother -in -law, oh they had a lot of land. And I went to shock and grain for him, and you know, a man does good when he keeps up with one binder, shock and grain. You don't know much about binders though, do you?

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Just seeing the pictures of them.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Yeah, yeah, well, but it drops up out bundled, you have to pick them up and shock them. And that's a man's job, when a man does that, he's done a hard day's work.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: You know what they had me do?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Keep up with two binders. Yeah, and I was just about 18 or 19 years old, and because they knew I'd do it, they had me do it. They said, take it easy, do what you ought to, but they just had me do it, yeah. And things like that, when I think of it, there's not very many people who'd have a boy doing that, but he was a good fella every other way too, everybody liked him. He just took advantage of you. Yeah, yeah, yeah.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: Boy, that sounds tough.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Yeah.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Well. So many young fellas, when they become 21 years old or older, was coming west to lower then. So, the fellas back in the long -term times, they liked it too. Of course, it was more wild and wooly then too, but they liked it a lot better than they did back east.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: I don't know if it's a certain kind of people that like it out here, and there's others that don't want to change, or what the reason is that some people come out and some people stay.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Yeah, but I tell you, there's not very many people that were born back east and come out here and live here a while. They don't want to go back. I think the percentage is pretty small.

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: But, I never did see the time that I ever wanted to live. After World War I, my wife and I were married back east and we stayed back every year or two afterwards. And I never had, we'd go back and visit quite a while. I'd like to go back and visit.

speaker: Sam Schrager
words: But you wouldn't want to live there?

speaker: Albert Pierce
words: Oh no, I wouldn't want it at all. I'm sure.
